summary: Stuart Russell discusses the evolution of AI, highlighting the successes and fundamental limitations of deep learning. He warns that the current trajectory of building superhuman AI without inherent safety guarantees risks human loss of control. To prevent this, he advocates for 'provably beneficial AI' based on assistance games, where AI is designed to be explicitly uncertain about human objectives and therefore remains helpful and controllable.

principles.statement: AI should be designed to solve the problem of helpfulness, not just intelligence.
principles.rationale: If we build systems more intelligent than us, we must ensure they act in our best interests. By making them explicitly uncertain about what those interests are, they are incentivized to remain under human control and allow themselves to be switched off.
principles.source_quote: We don't really want to solve the problem of intelligence. What we want is to solve the problem of helpfulness, right? Of AI systems that are very very intelligent but specifically intelligent in a way that is helpful to us.

principles.statement: AI must be safe by design from the beginning, not patched after the fact.
principles.rationale: Trying to fix the problems of unsafe AI after it has been built is merely a 'sticking plaster.' To retain power over entities more powerful than us, the core mathematical problem they solve must guarantee they are safe and beneficial.
principles.source_quote: So, we should stop talking about making AI safe, which is a after the-act sticking plaster trying to fix the problems of unsafe AI, and instead make AI that is safe by design from the beginning.

principles.statement: Deep learning's vast data requirements stem from its restricted representational capability.
principles.rationale: Deep learning relies on large circuits that are linear time computing systems. For complex computational problems, the circuit size and the data required to train it must blow up exponentially, unlike humans who can learn concepts from a single example.
principles.source_quote: the weakness of our current approach to AI is the vast data requirements in order to achieve even approximate representations of these difficult concepts.

frameworks.name: Assistance Games
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing the core mathematical objective of an AI system to ensure it remains provably beneficial and under human control.
steps: Set the AI's objective to act in the best interests of humans.
Ensure the AI is explicitly uncertain about what those human interests are.
Allow the AI to behave in ways that maintain human control (e.g., allowing itself to be switched off) while it figures out what humans want.
frameworks.source_quote: You set the objective to be that they have to act in the best interests of humans. But initially those machines are explicitly uncertain about what those human interests are.

mental_models.name: The AI System as a Box
mental_models.description: Viewing an AI system as a box where sensory input comes in and is processed to produce behavior. The history of AI is essentially the history of trying different ways to 'fill that box' (e.g., Fortran programs, symbolic logic, probabilistic programs, or deep learning circuits).
mental_models.example: Deep learning fills the box with a big circuit of up to trillions of elements connected by adjustable strengths, optimized via stochastic gradient descent.

mental_models.name: The AI Winter
mental_models.description: An analogy to nuclear winter, representing a prolonged period of frozen funding, interest, and research in a field after it fails to deliver on overhyped promises.
mental_models.example: The period from 1988 to the late 1990s after expert systems were perceived to have failed, resulting in no investment and empty classes.

heuristics: A machine is intelligent to the extent that its actions can be expected to achieve its objectives.
There are really only two options for the human race. We can build safe AI or we can have no AI at all.

signature_quotes.text: We don't really want to solve the problem of intelligence. What we want is to solve the problem of helpfulness.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the shift in objective needed to maintain control over superhuman AI.

signature_quotes.text: There are really only two options for the human race. We can build safe AI or we can have no AI at all.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting the binary choice humanity faces regarding the development of AGI.

signature_quotes.text: So, we should stop talking about making AI safe, which is a after the-act sticking plaster trying to fix the problems of unsafe AI, and instead make AI that is safe by design from the beginning.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding his argument for provably beneficial AI.

signature_quotes.text: A machine is intelligent to the extent that its actions can be expected to achieve its objectives.
signature_quotes.context: Defining artificial intelligence at the beginning of his talk.

anti_patterns.description: Treating AI safety as an after-the-fact patch or 'sticking plaster'.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Trying to fix the problems of an unsafe AI after it has been built does not work for superhuman systems. If the core mathematical problem it solves is unsafe, humans will lose control.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on deep learning by shoveling tons of data into a network and hoping for the best.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Deep learning circuits are linear time computing systems that require exponential data for complex tasks. They lack common sense and representational capability, leading to extreme brittleness in unusual circumstances (e.g., self-driving cars driving into wet cement).
